sections.title: 1. N+1 Query Patterns
sections.content:
**Not applicable** — This project uses an in-memory `MemoryStateStore` (map-based), not a database. No ORM/DAL patterns that would cause N+1 issues.

---

sections.title: A. SLA Parsing — `parseSLA` called on every retry
B. Priority Calculation — `getPriority` recalculates on every sort
C. Contract Lookup in `ValidateInput`/`ValidateOutput`
sections.content:
**File**: `executor.go:203-236, 238-272`

Both methods acquire `RLock`, then do map lookup and iteration. The contract schema doesn't change at runtime.

**Fix**: Use `sync.RWMutex` properly — reads already do `RLock`, which is correct. But consider caching validated schemas if schema count grows large.

---

sections.title: A. Busy-Wait Loop in `runTaskLoop` — CPU Spinning
sections.content:
**File**: `orchestrator.go:129-137`

```go
available := o.workerLimit - len(o.workerSem)
if available <= 0 {
    select {
    case <-ctx.Done():
        return
    case <-time.After(100 * time.Millisecond):  // ← This is inside select, fires immediately if nothing else
    }
    continue
}
```

**Issue**: `time.After` creates a timer on every iteration when `available <= 0`. Under high load, this creates many timer objects.

**Fix**: Use a single ticker or channel-based signaling:
```go
var timer *time.Timer
if timer == nil {
    timer = time.NewTimer(100 * time.Millisecond)
}
select {
case <-ctx.Done():
    return
case <-o.taskSubmitted:
case <-timer.C:
    timer.Reset(100 * time.Millisecond)
}
```

sections.title: B. Human Executor Busy-Wait Polling
sections.content:
**File**: `dispatcher.go:133-174`

```go
pollInterval := 100 * time.Millisecond  // Fixed 100ms polling
for {
    status, err := d.humanExecutor.GetCallStatus(task.TaskID)
    // ...
    time.Sleep(pollInterval)  // Always 100ms regardless of backoff
}
```

**Issue**: Fixed 100ms poll interval even if the call is likely to take longer. No exponential backoff or adaptive polling.

**Fix**: Implement exponential backoff with a maximum interval:
```go
pollInterval := 100 * time.Millisecond
maxInterval := 5 * time.Second
for {
    // ... check status
    time.Sleep(pollInterval)
    pollInterval = min(pollInterval * 2, maxInterval)
}
```

---

sections.title: A. Circular Dependency Detection — No Memoization
B. `GetAllTasks` — Deep Copy Every Time
C. `GetDependencyGraph` — Unnecessary Copy
sections.content:
**File**: `scheduler.go:276-291`

```go
result[id] = deps  // Creates new slice for every task
```

**Issue**: Creates new slices when original dependencies are already immutable after task submission.

**Fix**: Return read-only view or use slice header sharing.

---

sections.title: 5. Summary of Actionable Items (Priority Order)
sections.content:
| Priority | Issue | Location | Impact |
|----------|-------|----------|--------|
| **High** | Busy-wait with `time.After` in `runTaskLoop` | orchestrator.go:131 | CPU/memory under load |
| **High** | Fixed 100ms polling in human executor | dispatcher.go:173 | Unnecessary wakeups |
| **Medium** | `parseSLA` called every retry | orchestrator.go:170 | CPU waste on string parsing |
| **Medium** | `getPriority` recalculates on every sort | scheduler.go:184-186 | Redundant Atoi calls |
| **Low** | `GetAllTasks` deep copies everything | scheduler.go:247-273 | GC pressure |
| **Low** | Circular dependency has no memoization | scheduler.go:59 | O(n) per submit |

---

**Note**: This is a Go CLI project with no React components, so "unnecessary re-renders in React" is not applicable. The codebase is well-structured with proper interface/implementation separation and uses Go stdlib exclusively in core modules.

codeBlocks.code: result[id] = deps  // Creates new slice for every task

rawOutputPreview:
Based on my analysis, this is a **Go CLI project** (not React), so there are no React re-renders to analyze. Here's my performance analysis:

---

## Performance Optimization Report

### 1. N+1 Query Patterns
**Not applicable** — This project uses an in-memory `MemoryStateStore` (map-based), not a database. No ORM/DAL patterns that would cause N+1 issues.

---

### 2. Caching Opportunities

#### A. SLA Parsing — `parseSLA` called on every retry
**File**: `orchestrator.go:261-282`

```go
// parseSLA extracts SLA metadata. Missing keys return zero values (use defaults).
func parseSLA(metadata map[string]string) (timeoutMs int, maxRetries int, ...) {
    // Called every retry attempt, but SLA metadata is immutable
```

**Issue**: `parseSLA` does `strconv.Atoi` conversions on every retry attempt for the same `task.Metadata`. SLA metadata never changes during execution.

**Fix**: Cache parsed SLA on first call:
```go
type parsedSLA struct {
    timeoutMs   int
    maxRetries  int
    failureClass string
    backoff     string
    parsed      bool
}

// In executeTask, cache SLA parsing:
slaOnce := sync.Once{}
var sla parsedSLA
slaOnce.Do(func() {
    sla.timeoutMs, sla.maxRetries, sla.failureClass, sla.backoff = parseSLA(task.Metadata)
    sla.parsed = true
})
```

#### B. Priority Calculation — `getPriority` recalculates on every sort
**File**: `scheduler.go:214-227`

```go
// Called during every GetReadyTasks sort
sort.SliceStable(candidates, func(i, j int) bool {
    return getPriority(candidates[i]) > getPriority(candidates[j])
})
```

**Fix**: Pre-calculate and cache priority in task struct or memoize per call.

#### C. Contract Lookup in `ValidateInput`/`ValidateOutput`
**File**: `executor.go:203-236, 238-272`

Both methods acquire `RLock`, then do map lookup and iteration. The contract schema doesn't change at runtime.

**Fix**: Use `sync.RWMutex` properly — reads already do `RLock`, which is correct. But consider caching validated schemas if schema count grows la
